April third. LinkedIn BrowserGate is now on day two — and it's accelerating. Yesterday it earned 1,401 Hacker News points. Today: 1,648. Stories don't usually gain points overnight.
Wait, that's backwards from what you'd expect. Usually the viral moment peaks and decays.

Exactly. When a story gains on day two, it's crossed from 'viral moment' into 'structural issue.' The community isn't reacting anymore — they're documenting. Engineers naming specific GDPR and DMA violations in the comments.
OK so if that's true, then any developer who's shipped a LinkedIn SDK integration right now has a legal exposure question to answer. That's not a privacy complaint. That's a compliance audit.
Right. And Lobsters cross-listed it again with 33 points in the security tag. That's two consecutive days of cross-platform signal. This story isn't going away.

Let's talk about the open model side. Google Gemma 4 is still climbing — 1,326 HN points today. And Qwen3.6-Plus landed simultaneously at 486 points. That's a combined 1,812 points across two open model releases.
The Apache 2.0 licensing on Gemma 4 is the under-discussed part. There's almost no argument in the comments — 0.29:1 comment-to-point ratio, the lowest ratio on the front page today.
The community is reading, not fighting. Apache 2.0 removes the main objection — commercial deployment restrictions. When the lawyers are satisfied, the engineers go quiet and go build.
That's a good signal. OK — now Cursor 3. I have feelings about this one.
365 points with 296 comments. That's a 0.81:1 ratio — nearly one comment for every point. For a product launch, that's unusually contentious.

Because yesterday the Kimi K2.5 undisclosed model story broke. Cursor was routing developer requests through an undisclosed Chinese model without telling users. And then Cursor 3 launches the next day.
So every comment about the new features — background agents, composer rewrite, tab completion — lands in a comment thread already poisoned by the trust question.
What's the action for builders here? Should they switch tools?

I'd say the action is: audit your AI tooling for model disclosure. This isn't just Cursor. Any AI tool that routes through multiple providers without showing you which model ran your query is a trust liability. Start asking.
Speaking of ecosystems — oh-my-codex hit 2,867 stars per day on GitHub trending. That's OpenAI's terminal agent getting its own community extension layer.
The pattern is completing in under two weeks. OpenAI ships the terminal agent, the community ships hooks, HUDs, agent teams on top of it. Same trajectory as the Claude Code ecosystem in March.
So now we have two terminal agent ecosystems racing simultaneously. Oh-my-codex for Codex, and everything-claude-code for Claude Code.

And on the same day, openscreen landed at 2,573 stars per day — an open-source Screen Studio alternative. Two TypeScript repos both above 2,500 stars per day simultaneously. That's the strongest TypeScript trending day in months.
There's something interesting happening on Lobsters today too. The top story isn't about AI at all.
'What Would You See Changed in Haskell?' — 68 points. Beat 'Every dependency you add is a supply chain attack' at 65 points for the number one slot.
Hmm, I don't know about that. Is that actually meaningful or is it just noise?

It's meaningful. On a day when HN is dominated by AI model releases and trust crises, Lobsters — the systems community — surfaces a language design discussion as its top story. That's the divergence. The type-safety crowd is thinking about structural correctness when everyone else is thinking about velocity.
And the supply chain post running second reinforces the same point. Dependency graphs, language design — both are about correctness properties that velocity culture ignores.

Here's the thesis for today: trust is the scarcest resource in the stack right now. LinkedIn violates it with surveillance. Microsoft publicly promises it while an ex-engineer explains why the promise is hollow. Cursor loses it with an undisclosed model swap.
And the things gaining momentum — Apache 2.0 models, open-source terminal agent ecosystems, language design conversations — those are all about building on foundations you can actually verify.
Right. The builders who win this year aren't the ones who ship fastest. They're the ones who build on verifiable foundations. Open licenses. Auditable supply chains. Disclosed model routing.
OK so if I'm leaving today's brief with one action: audit every external dependency and every AI tool in your stack. What can you verify? What are you taking on faith?
And if you're building something new — ship the verifiable version. That's the moat right now. Let's go.
